title: Angle-Beam Examination: Evaluating Results and Reporting
narrationScript: An angle beam echo tells you three things if you know how to read it. How far the sound traveled, how strong the reflection was, and, with a little trigonometry, exactly where the reflector sits in the weld. This lesson turns screen readings into positions, positions into evaluations, and evaluations into a report with a sketch another technician could follow. Start with location. The calibrated screen gives you the sound path, the distance the shear wave traveled along its angled line. Two simple equations convert it. Surface distance from the index point to a spot directly above the reflector equals sound path times the sine of the refracted angle. Depth below the scanning surface equals sound path times the cosine of the angle, but only while the beam is still on its first leg, heading down toward the far wall. Once the beam has bounced, you are in the second leg, and the geometry folds. Depth then equals two times the thickness minus sound path times cosine of the angle. So the first question for every indication is, which leg am I in. Compare the sound path to the leg length, thickness divided by cosine of the angle. Shorter than one leg, first leg, use the direct formula. Between one and two leg lengths, second leg, use the folded one. Work the example we will keep all lesson. Half inch plate, sixty degree wedge, and an echo at one point four inches of sound path. One leg is one inch, so this is the second leg. Depth is two times one half, which is one, minus one point four times the cosine of sixty, which is zero point seven. That leaves zero point three inches deep, an upper third reflector. Surface distance is one point four times sine of sixty, about one point two one inches from the index point. Mark the index point, measure forward, and you can put your finger on top of the reflector. Plotting is also how you unmask geometry. A butt weld has legitimate reflectors that are not flaws. The root bead catches the first leg right at half skip and plots at the far surface on the weld centerline. The crown catches the second leg at full skip and plots at the scanning surface. Counterbore in piping plots at the far surface, offset from the centerline. The test is always the same. Plot the signal. If it lands on a surface where the drawing says metal is supposed to be, suspect geometry, confirm from the opposite side, check whether grinding the area or damping with a wet finger moves the signal, and only then call it nonrelevant. A real flaw plots inside the weld volume or at a fusion line, and it usually shows from both sides of the weld while geometry often will not. Next, evaluation. Maximize the echo, read its amplitude against the distance amplitude correction curve at that sound path, and express it as a percentage of the curve. The procedure sets the levels. A common scheme evaluates anything over twenty percent of the curve, records at fifty percent, and applies the acceptance table at one hundred percent, where length usually decides. Length comes from the six decibel drop. Find the peak, halve the amplitude, and the index point marks the end of the reflector. Walk to the other end the same way. Measure the length between the marks along the weld axis, and record the maximum amplitude location, its depth, and the leg it was found in. Then the report. Everything a straight beam report carries comes along, part, procedure and revision, instrument, search unit, calibration block, couplant, reference gain, calibration confirmations. Angle beam adds its own essentials. The measured refracted angle, not just the stencil. The beam index point verification. The surfaces and sides scanned, the scan band, and any areas you could not reach. For each indication, the sound path, the leg, the surface distance, the depth, the position along the weld from the datum, the offset from centerline, the length, and the amplitude as a percentage of the reference curve. And the plotting sketch. A clean cross section view with the beam drawn to the reflector, plus a plan view locating it along the weld, says more than a page of numbers, and most procedures require exactly that. Sign it with your name, level, and date, and write the disposition the criteria dictate, not the one the schedule wants. Plot honestly, size honestly, and hand in a report that can stand alone. That is the whole job.
imagePrompt: A technician holds a steel rule from a numbered datum stamp along a pipe weld to the marked index point of an angle-beam probe, while sketching the indication position on a clipboard report form beside a flaw detector.

sections.heading: From Sound Path to Position
sections.body:
The calibrated screen hands you one number — the **sound path** (s), the distance traveled along the beam. Trigonometry converts it to a position (refracted angle θ, thickness T):

- **Surface distance** from the beam index point: s × sin θ
- **Depth, first leg** (before the back-wall bounce): s × cos θ
- **Depth, second leg** (after the bounce): 2T − s × cos θ

The leg question comes first. One leg of sound path is T/cos θ. If s is shorter than one leg, the beam is still heading down — use the direct depth formula. If s falls between one and two leg lengths, the beam has bounced — use the folded formula.

Worked example: T = 0.5 in., θ = 60°, echo at s = 1.40 in. One leg = 0.5 ÷ 0.5 = **1.00 in.**, so this is the **second leg**. Depth = 2(0.5) − 1.40 × 0.5 = **0.30 in.** below the scanning surface; surface distance = 1.40 × 0.866 = **1.21 in.** ahead of the index point. Mark the index point, measure 1.21 in. forward, and your finger is on top of a reflector 0.30 in. down — in the upper third of the weld.

sections.caption: Plotting an angle-beam indication: sound path and refracted angle give surface distance and depth, with the depth formula folding after the back-wall bounce.

sections.heading: Geometry or Flaw? Plot Before You Judge
sections.body:
A sound weld still returns echoes, and mistaking them for flaws — or flaws for them — is the classic angle-beam error. The regulars:

- **Root bead.** Caught in the first leg near half skip; plots **at the far surface on the weld centerline**. A normal root reinforcement gives a clean, repeatable signal along the whole joint.
- **Crown reinforcement.** Caught in the second leg near full skip; plots **at the scanning surface** over the weld.
- **Counterbore or mismatch** in piping: plots at the far surface, offset from the centerline, typically running parallel to the weld.

The discipline is always the same: **plot the signal**. If it lands where the drawing puts metal surfaces, suspect geometry — then confirm. Scan from the **opposite side** (geometry often disappears or moves predictably; flaws usually persist), compare the location against the joint detail, and where permitted, damp the suspect surface with a couplant-wetted finger and watch the signal respond. Genuine discontinuities plot **within the weld volume or along fusion lines** — lack of sidewall fusion sits on the bevel line, incomplete penetration at the root centerline with a strong, sharp response from both sides.

sections.title: Evaluate against the curve, not against your nerves
sections.body: Amplitude is read **as a percentage of the DAC** at the indication's sound path. A common code scheme: investigate indications over **20% DAC**, record at **50% DAC**, and apply the acceptance table at **100% DAC**, where measured **length** typically decides acceptance. Your procedure states the exact levels — the screen never does. Record what you measured; the governing code, not the production schedule, writes the disposition.

sections.heading: Sizing Length Along the Weld
sections.body:
Once an indication crosses the recording level, characterize it completely:

1. **Maximize** the echo — small raster and swivel movements until the amplitude peaks. Record the peak amplitude (% DAC), its sound path, leg, computed depth, and surface distance.
2. **Length by 6 dB drop.** From the peak, track the probe along the weld axis until the amplitude falls to **half the peak value**; the beam centerline marks one end of the reflector. Repeat in the other direction. The distance between marks is the recorded length.
3. **Locate from the datum.** Measure the position of the maximum-amplitude point and both ends along the weld from the **zero reference** (datum) the procedure establishes, and note the offset from the weld centerline and the side scanned from.
4. **Cross-check.** Where access allows, confirm position and amplitude from the second side; a reflector that reads very differently from two directions is telling you about its **orientation** — information the reviewing Level II or III needs.

Intermittent responses get start-stop positions; the procedure defines when neighboring indications are evaluated as one continuous reflector.

sections.caption: Report plotting sketch: the indication is fixed by datum distance, centerline offset, depth, and 6 dB drop length, with the indication data table carrying amplitude as a percentage of DAC.

sections.heading: The Angle-Beam Test Report
sections.body:
Everything required of a straight-beam report carries over: part and material identification, procedure and revision, acceptance criteria reference, instrument and search-unit identification (make, model, serial, frequency, element size), calibration block ID, couplant, surface condition, reference gain, scanning gain, and every calibration confirmation with its time and result.

Angle-beam work adds its own essential entries:

- **Measured refracted angle** (not the wedge stencil) and the verified **beam index point**
- **Wedge identification**, examination surface(s), and **side(s) of the weld scanned**, with any inaccessible lengths documented
- Scan band dimensions and coverage achieved (legs used, both-side coverage or single-side justification)
- For each indication: **datum distance, centerline offset, sound path, leg, depth, surface distance, length, maximum amplitude in % DAC**, and the classification or disposition assigned by the evaluating level
- The **plotting sketch**: a cross-section showing the beam path to the reflector and a plan view locating it along the joint

The repeatability test still governs: a second qualified technician, handed only your report, should find the same reflector within a probe-width. Sign with printed name, certification level, signature, and date.

sections.caption: Example indication log entries from an angle-beam weld examination report.
rows: Plots at root surface — geometry, verified side B

sections.heading: Habits of a Defensible Angle-Beam Report
sections.body:
Field practices that keep the paper as good as the scan:

- **Mark the index point on the wedge and the position on the part** before reading the rule — measuring from the wedge front adds a constant error to every location.
- **Use the measured angle in every calculation.** A 60° wedge verified at 61° plots noticeably differently by the second leg of thick material.
- **Sketch in cross-section first.** Drawing the beam to the reflector exposes leg mistakes immediately — a depth of 0.7 in. in 0.5 in. plate is impossible and the sketch shows it.
- **Record the leg** for every indication. The same screen position can be a deep first-leg reflector or a shallow second-leg one; the leg entry removes the ambiguity for reviewers.
- **Document the negative findings too**: lengths examined with no recordable indications, and any weld length not examined and why.
- **Never average away a hot spot.** The maximum amplitude point is recorded where it is, even if the rest of the indication is quiet.

Plot honestly, size honestly, and the report defends itself.

figures.title: Plotting depth and surface distance, first and second leg
Angle-beam report plotting sketch and indication log

interactive.title: First Leg or Second Leg?
interactive.intro: For each echo, compute one leg length (T/cos θ) and compare it with the sound path shown on the screen. Classify the reflector: is the beam still on its first leg, or has it bounced off the back wall? Reference: cos 45° = 0.707, cos 60° = 0.500, cos 70° = 0.342.

items.label: T = 0.500 in., 60° wedge, sound path 0.80 in.
items.why: One leg = 0.500 ÷ 0.500 = 1.00 in. The 0.80 in. path is shorter, so the beam has not yet reached the back wall: depth = 0.80 × 0.5 = 0.40 in.

items.label: T = 0.500 in., 60° wedge, sound path 1.60 in.
items.why: One leg = 1.00 in.; 1.60 in. falls between one and two legs. Depth = 2(0.5) − 1.60 × 0.5 = 0.20 in. below the scanning surface.

items.label: T = 0.375 in., 70° wedge, sound path 0.90 in.
items.why: One leg = 0.375 ÷ 0.342 ≈ 1.10 in. A 0.90 in. path is still first leg: depth = 0.90 × 0.342 ≈ 0.31 in.

items.label: T = 0.375 in., 70° wedge, sound path 1.80 in.
items.why: One leg ≈ 1.10 in., two legs ≈ 2.19 in.; 1.80 in. is in the second leg. Depth = 0.750 − 1.80 × 0.342 ≈ 0.13 in. — a near-surface reflector.

items.label: T = 1.000 in., 45° wedge, sound path 1.20 in.
items.why: One leg = 1.000 ÷ 0.707 ≈ 1.41 in. The 1.20 in. path is first leg: depth = 1.20 × 0.707 ≈ 0.85 in.

items.label: T = 1.000 in., 45° wedge, sound path 2.40 in.
items.why: One leg ≈ 1.41 in., two legs ≈ 2.83 in.; 2.40 in. has bounced. Depth = 2.000 − 2.40 × 0.707 ≈ 0.30 in.

items.label: T = 0.750 in., 60° wedge, sound path 1.40 in.
items.why: One leg = 0.750 ÷ 0.500 = 1.50 in. At 1.40 in. the beam is just short of the back wall: depth = 1.40 × 0.5 = 0.70 in., near the far surface.

items.label: T = 0.750 in., 60° wedge, sound path 2.70 in.
items.why: One leg = 1.50 in., two legs = 3.00 in.; 2.70 in. is second leg. Depth = 1.500 − 2.70 × 0.5 = 0.15 in. below the scanning surface.

checkQuestions.question: With a 70° wedge (cos 70° = 0.342) on 0.750 in. plate, an echo appears at 1.50 in. sound path. One leg is 2.19 in., so the reflector depth is:
checkQuestions.explanation: The sound path (1.50 in.) is less than one leg (0.750 ÷ 0.342 ≈ 2.19 in.), so the beam is on its first leg and depth = s × cos θ = 1.50 × 0.342 ≈ 0.51 in.

checkQuestions.question: An indication peaks at 2.00 in. sound path with a 60° wedge (sin 60° = 0.866). How far ahead of the beam index point is the reflector along the surface?
checkQuestions.explanation: Surface distance = s × sin θ = 2.00 × 0.866 ≈ 1.73 in. — measured from the verified index point, which is why a worn wedge corrupts every location.

checkQuestions.question: T = 0.625 in., 45° wedge (cos 45° = 0.707), echo at 1.30 in. sound path. One leg is 0.88 in., so the depth is:
checkQuestions.explanation: 1.30 in. exceeds one leg (0.625 ÷ 0.707 ≈ 0.88 in.), so the beam has bounced: depth = 2T − s·cos θ = 1.250 − 1.30 × 0.707 ≈ 0.33 in. below the scanning surface.

checkQuestions.question: A repeatable signal appears at almost exactly full skip distance and plots at the scanning surface directly over the weld. Before recording it as a flaw, you should suspect:
options: a reflection from the weld crown reinforcement — verify by plotting, opposite-side scanning, and surface damping where permitted
a lamination in the base plate
checkQuestions.explanation: At full skip the second leg returns to the scanning surface, where the crown lives. Geometry is confirmed — never assumed — by plotting against the joint detail, checking from the other side, and damping the suspect surface.

checkQuestions.question: Which entry group appears on an angle-beam report but not on a straight-beam report?
options: Instrument make, model, and serial number
Measured refracted angle, beam index point verification, leg, and side of weld scanned
Examiner name, level, signature, and date
checkQuestions.explanation: The angle-specific data — verified angle and index point, the leg each indication was found in, and the scanning side(s) — are what let a reviewer re-plot every reflector; the other entries are common to both report types.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, UT Technique Course §4.3 (evaluation of results), §4.4 (test reports)
Codes such as ASME BPVC Section V, Article 4 (DAC evaluation levels, examination records) and weld acceptance tables in construction codes (general practice)
Angle-beam trigonometry (reference): surface distance = s·sin θ; depth = s·cos θ (leg 1) or 2T − s·cos θ (leg 2)
SNT-TC-1A (recording vs. evaluation duties by certification level, general framing)
